history.hpi: 22-year-old female found by her roommate surrounded by empty bottles of prescription benzodiazepines and an empty fifth of vodka. Roommate returned from work and found the patient unresponsive on the couch. Time of ingestion unknown but likely within the past 2-3 hours based on roommate leaving at 15:00.
pastMedical: Prior suicide attempt (2 years ago, medication overdose)
history.lastOralIntake: Unknown timing; empty pill bottles and vodka bottle at scene
history.events: Roommate found patient unresponsive on couch, called 911. Bystander (roommate) administered naloxone 4 mg IN from her workplace kit with no effect. Police on scene confirmed no obvious signs of trauma.

scene.dispatch: Medic 9, respond code 3 to 815 College Heights Drive apartment 2, for a 22-year-old female, unresponsive, possible overdose. Police on scene.
scene.location: Second-floor college apartment. Patient supine on the living room couch with an arm hanging off. Access is straightforward.
scene.safety: Scene secured by police. Note unsecured prescription bottles. Consider BSI for possible vomitus.
scene.bystanders: Roommate is distraught but providing information. Hands the crew the empty pill bottles and vodka. Police officer present.
visualCues: Young adult female supine on couch, unresponsive
Two empty alprazolam bottles (1 mg tabs, original fill of 60)
Empty fifth of vodka on the floor
Vomitus on the couch cushion and on the patient's shirt
Gurgling respirations audible from the doorway
Pinpoint pupils — likely artifact of alprazolam, not opioid
Spent intranasal naloxone device on coffee table

phases.name: Arrival — Unresponsive with Soiled Airway
phases.description: Crew finds an unresponsive young woman with critical airway compromise from emesis pooling and lost protective reflexes. Narcan has been given bystander without effect — consistent with non-opioid toxidrome. Respirations are slow and shallow, SpO2 is 82%, and the patient has no gag reflex. This is an airway protection case.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Gurgling respirations with emesis pooling in the posterior oropharynx; no gag reflex; jaw relaxed
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Slow, shallow respirations at 6/min; minimal tidal volume; no accessory muscle use
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Weak but palpable radial pulse; cool peripheries
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Small (2 mm), minimally reactive — consistent with sedative-hypnotic, not pathognomonic of opioid
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Flaccid with occasional localized withdrawal to deep noxious stimulus
otherFindings: Two empty alprazolam bottles at the scene (roughly 120 tablets)
Sedative-hypnotic toxidrome: CNS depression + hypoventilation, normal BP
Bystander naloxone already administered with no effect

expectedActions.action: BSI/PPE with attention to vomitus; confirm scene safe with law enforcement
Aggressive oropharyngeal suctioning with Yankauer; have suction available throughout
Place patient in left-lateral recovery position temporarily while preparing for definitive airway
Place oropharyngeal airway (absent gag reflex) and begin 2-person BVM with PEEP 5 cmH2O
Check blood glucose
Identify agents: alprazolam (benzodiazepine) and ethanol — sedative-hypnotic toxidrome. Collect all pill bottles to bring to ED.
Do NOT administer flumazenil — contraindicated in mixed ingestion and may precipitate seizures in benzo-dependent patients
Recognize that additional naloxone will not help — pupils are not opioid-pattern and bystander naloxone had no effect. Do not chase it further.
Apply cardiac monitor, SpO2, waveform capnography, 12-lead EKG (look for widened QRS from SSRI/other sodium channel effect)
Establish IV access

phases.name: RSI for Airway Protection
phases.description: Despite BVM and suction, patient has recurrent emesis and cannot protect her airway. GCS remains 6. Definitive airway is indicated for airway protection from aspiration, not because BVM cannot oxygenate. RSI is appropriate even with relatively stable hemodynamics.
phases.triggerCondition: Recurrent emesis, inability to protect airway, GCS ≤8

transitions.condition: Successful RSI with confirmed tube placement
Failure to secure airway promptly leads to aspiration pneumonitis

clinicalPresentation.airway: OPA in place; recurrent emesis despite suctioning
otherFindings: SpO2 has risen to 92% on BVM but remains borderline
EtCO2 trending down from 62 with assisted ventilation

expectedActions.action: Verbalize RSI plan with awareness of aspiration risk — have suction immediately accessible
Administer NS bolus 500 mL prior to induction to pre-load hemodynamics (hypotension risk from sedatives + ethanol on board)
Continue 2-person BVM preoxygenation + PEEP 5 cmH2O with apneic oxygenation NC 15 LPM
Administer Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV push (100 mg for 65 kg patient) as induction agent; ketamine preferred over midazolam which would add to existing benzo load
expectedActions.rationale: Patient has already taken significant benzodiazepines. Additional midazolam for induction would worsen hemodynamics and compound sedation. Etomidate 0.2-0.3 mg/kg (13-20 mg) is also reasonable.

expectedActions.action: Administer Rocuronium 1 mg/kg IV push (65 mg for 65 kg patient)
Video laryngoscopy with SALAD technique (suction-assisted) to manage ongoing secretions; 7.0-7.5 mm ETT
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography, bilateral breath sounds, absent epigastric sounds

phases.name: Post-Intubation Supportive Care
phases.description: Tube confirmed. Supportive care for sedative-hypnotic overdose: ventilation, gastric decompression, fluid resuscitation if needed, and prompt transport. Poison control contact recommended.

clinicalPresentation.airway: 7.0 mm ETT at 21 cm, cuff inflated, secured

expectedActions.action: Minimal post-intubation sedation: Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg (65 mcg) IV for analgesia; avoid additional benzodiazepines given ingestion
expectedActions.rationale: Patient already has significant benzodiazepine load — additional midazolam is not needed for sedation and would worsen hypotension risk.

expectedActions.action: Ventilate at 12 breaths/min, 6-8 mL/kg IBW, EtCO2 target 35-45 mmHg
Place orogastric tube for gastric decompression
Apply warm blankets — core temperature borderline and sedatives impair thermoregulation
Bring all pill bottles and the vodka bottle to the ED for toxicology
Contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) en route for guidance on mixed ingestion
Transport to appropriate ED with early notification

phases.name: Branch: Aspiration Pneumonitis from Delayed Intubation
phases.description: Crew chooses to continue BVM and defer intubation. Repeated vomiting leads to aspiration. The patient develops hypoxemia despite BVM and now has a soiled lower airway requiring intubation in a compromised state.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Gurgling with gastric contents in posterior pharynx despite intermittent suction
clinicalPresentation.breathing: BVM-assisted but with rhonchi and decreased compliance

expectedActions.action: Proceed with emergent RSI using SALAD technique; anticipate difficult visualization due to gastric contents
Deep endotracheal suctioning after tube placement
Recognize that earlier RSI would have prevented aspiration — this is the teachable moment

criticalActions: Recognize sedative-hypnotic toxidrome distinct from opioid overdose (naloxone won't work)
Place OPA and begin 2-person BVM with PEEP
Administer NS bolus pre-induction to mitigate hypotension risk
Perform RSI with Ketamine 1-2 mg/kg (100 mg) + Rocuronium 1 mg/kg (65 mg)
Confirm tube placement with waveform capnography
Minimal sedation post-intubation given on-board sedative load
expectedActions: Collect pill bottles and agents for the ED
12-lead EKG to screen for sodium channel effects
bonusActions: Articulate why additional naloxone is unhelpful
Choose ketamine over benzodiazepine-based induction in this patient

learningObjectives: Differentiate sedative-hypnotic toxidrome from opioid toxidrome
Apply airway protection principles in overdose with intact hemodynamics but lost reflexes
Understand contraindications for flumazenil in mixed ingestion
Anticipate and mitigate aspiration risk during RSI
Choose appropriate induction and paralytic agents in a patient with on-board sedatives
discussionQuestions: Why was flumazenil not administered even though the primary agent is a benzodiazepine?
Naloxone was given by a bystander with no effect — what did that tell you about the toxidrome?
How does SALAD technique change your approach to intubating a soiled airway?
Why might you choose ketamine over etomidate or midazolam in this patient?
How do you decide whether to intubate an overdose patient who is 'holding her own' on BVM?
commonPitfalls: Giving more naloxone because 'the pupils are pinpoint' without recognizing the sedative-hypnotic toxidrome
Considering flumazenil in a polypharmacy ingestion
Failing to bolus fluids pre-induction and causing post-sedative hypotension
Using midazolam for post-intubation sedation in an already benzo-overdosed patient
Delaying intubation because 'BVM is working' in a patient who cannot protect her airway
keyTakeaways: Airway protection is a distinct indication for intubation — not everyone who needs a tube is hypoxic
Flumazenil is not for prehospital use in mixed ingestions
The goal is supportive care until the drugs clear; don't get distracted chasing antidotes that don't apply
SALAD technique is essential for soiled airways
